text: In the life of every surgeon there will come a day in which an EEA stapler either fails or is not available and therefore in this video I'm covering how to do a fully hand-sewn end-to-end colonic anastomosis in a robotic fashion. And with the recent recall of Ethicon EEA staplers, although we have grown accustomed to and almost dependent upon, these things do have a failure rate and also it is imperative that we all have some knowledge of how to actually do these operations in an end-to-end fashion without the need for the actual stapler.

So I want to run down that in this video here, but first some schematics. First off, if you can see by this schematic drawing that involves two open ends, starting sutures at the mid portion of the back wall of the anastomosis. This is followed by the graphic on the left, an internal running suture in a full thickness manner, running in to out, in to out, from the back wall, two suture lines, one heading, in this case, in the north direction, and in this case, heading in the south direction. There's a transition stitch that has to occur once you get to the corners. So at one point, it's a full thickness from lumen to lumen, and you encircle and go lumen to lumen again, but at some point, you have to transition, and this diagram is depicted in which, on the, let's call it the colonic side on the right here, that once that stitch comes through the right, you then backwall it out and begin what's called your canal stitch. And so this is where the canal stitch is being formed on the anterior wall. And the canal basically runs in a vertical fashion. in line with the bowel. Same thing on this northern suture and same thing on the southern suture. And this is a schematic drawing of what that anterior canal suture line looks like and when it's completed at the end.

Alright, so let's run through an operative video demonstrating a hand-sewn colocolonic anastomosis. A little case context here. We are currently in the midst of an Ethicon recall of all EEA staplers and therefore the only ones that we have are a few on the shelf and knowing that this technique was something that I was interested in doing. I went ahead and shot this video in a patient that had a mid sigmoid endoscopic unresectable polyp. So what I've already done here is I've done the mobilization, done the ligation of the IMA, got everything skeletonized, and I've already had divided distally here sharply, and then divided the descending sigmoid junction proximally sharply as well.
The surgeon begins the procedure by using two V-lock sutures to suture the posterior wall of the anastomosis. The distal bowel is aligned with the proximal bowel at the posterior midline.

The surgeon locks the V-lock suture and cinches it down before beginning to run the northern suture. The suture is applied in a full thickness manner, sewing from lumen to lumen, in to out, then out to in, along the entire back wall of the anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully cinches down the V-lock suture after each throw, ensuring it is tight enough to remove slack and bury the tines without acting as a tourniquet.

The surgeon continues suturing from intraluminal to extraluminal on the distal limb and from extraluminal to intraluminal on the proximal limb, advancing approximately five millimeters with each stitch. The fenestrated bipolar instrument is used in the left hand for precision. The suturing progresses towards the anterior canal of the anastomosis.

The surgeon begins the second posterior suture line using a 3-0 V-lock suture with a V-20 needle, overlapping the initial northern suture line at the posterior midline. The suture is run intraluminally to extraluminally and vice versa, ensuring overlap due to the absence of tines at the distal end of the V-lock suture.

The surgeon continues full thickness suturing around the corner of the anastomosis, transitioning to the canal with a series of out-to-in and in-to-out stitches. The corners are manipulated with a dunk and roll technique to improve alignment, repeating the same process on the proximal limb.

The surgeon places the last few stitches in the ileorectal anastomosis, carefully transitioning the suture from out to in and in to out. The tension is adjusted on the third arm to ensure clear visibility of the final canal stitch, using a V-lock suture technique.

The surgeon ties the sutures together using VLOC, reinforcing the inter-suture line with running interrupted Lembert stitches. The endoscopic view shows the wide opening, with insufflation confirming no leaks. Additional interrupted stitches are visible in the background.
The anastomosis is visibly wide open, demonstrating a fully hand-sewn intracorporeal anastomosis in an end-to-end fashion.
